prompt: A technician replaces a failed compressor on a household window air conditioner. What is the maximum refrigerant charge that typically classifies this unit as a Type I small appliance?

options.text: 5 pounds or less of refrigerant
15 pounds or less of refrigerant
50 pounds or less of refrigerant
Any charge if the system is hermetic

explanation: EPA Type I covers small appliances that are manufactured, charged, and hermetically sealed in a factory with 5 pounds or less of refrigerant.

distractorExplanations.b: Fifteen pounds exceeds the Type I small-appliance charge limit.
distractorExplanations.c: Fifty pounds describes larger equipment outside Type I scope.
distractorExplanations.d: Hermetic construction alone does not remove the 5-pound charge cap for Type I classification.

prompt: After recovering refrigerant from a small appliance, where must the recovered refrigerant be sent?

options.text: To an EPA-certified reclaimer, distributor, or recycler approved to accept it
Directly to a municipal landfill if the charge is under 1 pound
Back into the atmosphere through a purge valve
Only to the original equipment manufacturer

explanation: Recovered refrigerant must be handled by certified reclaimers or approved recipients; intentional venting is prohibited.

distractorExplanations.b: Landfill disposal of refrigerant cylinders or vented refrigerant is not permitted.
distractorExplanations.d: Manufacturers are not the only approved destination for recovered refrigerant.

prompt: When adding R-410A to a residential split system, why must technicians use a tank with a reverse-threaded (left-hand) service valve?

options.text: To prevent accidental use of the wrong refrigerant on an R-410A system
Because R-410A cannot be stored in standard cylinders
To increase recovery speed on high-pressure systems
Because R-410A requires oxygen service fittings

explanation: R-410A systems use unique left-hand threads on service ports and cylinders to reduce cross-contamination with other refrigerants such as R-22.

distractorExplanations.b: R-410A is stored in standard refrigerant cylinders with left-hand fittings, not exotic containers.
distractorExplanations.c: Thread orientation is a safety and compatibility measure, not a recovery-speed feature.
distractorExplanations.d: R-410A does not use oxygen-service fittings; left-hand threads are the distinguishing feature.

prompt: A technician pressure-tests a repaired R-22 split system with dry nitrogen. When is it acceptable to release the nitrogen?

options.text: After confirming the test pressure is released to atmosphere in a ventilated area with no mixed refrigerant present
Only if refrigerant is vented first to speed up the test
Never — nitrogen must be recovered into a refrigerant cylinder
Only when the system charge is below 3 pounds

explanation: Dry nitrogen used for leak testing may be vented after the test if no regulated refrigerant is mixed with it and safe venting practices are followed.

distractorExplanations.b: Refrigerant must be recovered before testing; venting refrigerant is prohibited.
distractorExplanations.c: Nitrogen used alone for leak testing does not require recovery into refrigerant cylinders.
distractorExplanations.d: The small-charge exception does not govern nitrogen venting after a leak test.

prompt: Which leak rate threshold applies to commercial refrigeration equipment with more than 50 pounds of refrigerant under EPA regulations?

options.text: 20% annual leak rate before repair requirements apply
5% annual leak rate before repair requirements apply
0% — any detectable leak requires immediate system replacement
35% annual leak rate before repair requirements apply

explanation: EPA leak repair rules set category-specific thresholds; commercial refrigeration with more than 50 pounds is commonly cited at a 20% annual leak rate trigger for repair and recordkeeping.

distractorExplanations.b: Five percent applies to other appliance categories such as comfort cooling, not this commercial refrigeration threshold.
distractorExplanations.c: EPA rules require repair within specified timeframes at defined leak rates, not automatic replacement at any leak.
distractorExplanations.d: Thirty-five percent is not the commercial refrigeration threshold under EPA leak repair requirements.

prompt: Why must low-pressure chillers be kept under a slight positive pressure during extended shutdowns?

options.text: To prevent air and moisture from entering the system through leaks on the low side
To eliminate the need for oil changes
To allow direct venting of non-condensables without recovery
To convert the chiller to a high-pressure appliance

explanation: Low-pressure systems operate below atmospheric pressure on the evaporator side; positive pressure during shutdown reduces air and moisture ingress that causes acids and non-condensables.

distractorExplanations.b: Oil maintenance is still required; pressure control addresses contamination ingress, not lubrication intervals.
distractorExplanations.c: Non-condensables must still be handled without venting regulated refrigerant.
distractorExplanations.d: Operating pressure classification is fixed by equipment design, not shutdown pressurization.

prompt: When recovering refrigerant from a low-pressure centrifugal chiller, what is the typical first step before opening the machine?

options.text: Warm the refrigerant to drive vapor into the condenser and start recovery from the proper low-side access
Vent the purge unit directly to atmosphere
Add R-410A to raise system pressure above 100 psig
Remove all oil before any recovery begins

explanation: Low-pressure chiller recovery often requires controlled heating to boil refrigerant off the evaporator bundle and route vapor for recovery because the machine operates below atmospheric pressure on the low side.

distractorExplanations.b: Venting through the purge unit can release regulated refrigerant and violates recovery requirements.
distractorExplanations.c: Mixing incompatible refrigerants such as R-410A into a low-pressure chiller is improper and dangerous.
distractorExplanations.d: Oil removal is not the first recovery step; refrigerant must be recovered before major opening of the circuit.

prompt: A technician is leak testing a low-pressure chiller after replacing a tube bundle gasket. Which practice complies with EPA requirements?

options.text: Pressurize with dry nitrogen and soap-bubble test before pulling a deep vacuum and charging
Use controlled R-123 release to locate leaks by smell
Skip leak testing if the chiller passed operation last season
Vent the entire charge to atmosphere to speed gasket seating

explanation: After major repairs, technicians leak test with an inert trace gas such as nitrogen, then evacuate and charge; intentional refrigerant venting for leak detection is prohibited.

distractorExplanations.b: Using refrigerant release as a leak tracer violates venting prohibitions.
distractorExplanations.c: Prior season performance does not replace post-repair leak verification.
distractorExplanations.d: Venting the charge is illegal and unnecessary for gasket replacement.
